All reports
Fenn & Hart
PITCHPRO

Voice of Customer Report

What roofing contractors say about the software they use, in their own words, and what it tells PitchPro to claim.

Prepared by Fenn & Hart
June 2026

0. Executive Summary

Bottom line. Roofing contractors keep saying three things across every platform they review: keep my whole business in one place, stop charging me for every add-on, and give me a phone app and a QuickBooks sync that actually work. PitchPro has no customers yet, so every quote in this report comes from public reviews of the category and its competitors, not from PitchPro’s own buyers. That public record points the messaging at one place to plant the flag: roofing-native software built to run itself, with one predictable bill and no add-on surprises.

The customers who write these reviews want one system, not five. They love consolidation when they get it and they rage when a vendor sells it as one product and then bills it as ten. Justin H. says the win plainly: AccuLynx “eliminates the chaos of using multiple apps and keeps the whole team aligned.”9 Dennis C. names the pain on the other side: “Everything is an add-on, and you have to commit to the whole portion of that add-on in regard to price, even if using only bits and pieces of that.”7

The biggest gap between how vendors talk and how customers talk is the word “all-in-one.” Four of the top six competitors use it in their primary positioning.46 Customers never say “all-in-one.” They say “I have to double enter everything,”18 “the mobile app barely works,”19 and “I have to use JobNimbus for residential and another product for commercial…”12 They describe the job, not the category label.

The single highest-value language shift is to retire “all-in-one” and lead with the specific thing customers are begging for: a roofing-native system that runs the back office itself, bills one predictable price, and does the unglamorous plumbing (QuickBooks, mobile, photos) that funded incumbents keep breaking. Section 8 carries the moves that follow.

0.5 Evidence Base and Recommended Primary Research

PitchPro is pre-revenue. It has zero customers and zero first-party customer interviews, so there is no PitchPro buyer to quote.45 Every verbatim in this report is a public review of the roofing-software category and its named competitors: AccuLynx, JobNimbus, Roofr, Housecall Pro, CompanyCam, and EagleView, pulled from Capterra, G2, the Apple App Store, and named industry publications. These are category pains, the conditions of the market PitchPro is entering. They describe rival software, not PitchPro.

Public reviews tell you what the category resents and craves, and that has a ceiling. They cannot tell you which message makes a PitchPro prospect sign up, how a roofing owner’s emotions differ from his estimator’s at the moment of purchase, or how long a real buyer sits between first thought and decision. Those answers come from talking to buyers directly.

So the first strategic move in this report is to commission a primary-interview and case-study program, run as AI-led custom interviews with PitchPro’s first trial customers. Section 8 states it as the lead action. It would confirm or sharpen four things this report can only infer from the category: the emotional registers at purchase, the buyer-versus-user split, the real timeline from first thought to first use, and the forces that win or lose the deal. Treat the public record below as the starting map and the primary interviews as the survey that draws the real terrain.

1. Verbatim Customer Language

The themes below are named the way contractors name them in their reviews. Each is anchored in real quotes, and each note says how widely the pattern runs and whether it is specific to one vendor or shows up across the whole category.

1.1 “I have to double enter everything”

The deepest, most repeated pain in the category is fragmentation: data that lives in two systems, or no system, so the same information gets typed twice. It appears across AccuLynx, JobNimbus, and EagleView alike, which makes it a category condition, not one vendor’s bug.

“I have to double enter everything OR use a completely different estimating suite…. which I would rather not do.”18 Persona: Production Manager. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“I have to use JobNimbus for residential and another product for commercial as Nimbus doesnt do any job costing or vendor portals, or customer portals…. The new insights is AWFUL! The functionality is questionable at best… tons of wasted time fixing errors.”12 Persona: Roofing Owner / VP. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“The data sent to my estimate software does not send the data that is suggested for the material required. This requires me to have to open the document and read the suggested amount and transcribe that info into the estimate software. At the bottom of the document is a quick snapshot of the house in total foot for all rakes, eaves, perimeter, parapet walls and so forth. If this information was in the data of the report I could extract that data into my estimate software. At this time I have to copy that section for every report.”26 Persona: Project Manager / Estimator. Stage: Use & Succeed.

The flip side is how strongly they praise the rare moment it works. When one system holds everything, the relief is the headline of the review.

“I like that AccuLynx keeps all jobs, leads, documents, photos, notes, and communication inside one system. It eliminates the chaos of using multiple apps and keeps the whole team aligned.”9 Persona: Sales Manager / Roofing. Stage: Use & Succeed.

1.2 “Everything is an add-on”

Add-on pricing draws the angriest language in the entire set. Customers do not object to paying. They object to being sold a platform and then billed for each piece of it. This runs across AccuLynx, CompanyCam, and Housecall Pro, so it is a category-wide grievance any new entrant can exploit.

“Everything is an add-on, and you have to commit to the whole portion of that add-on in regard to price, even if using only bits and pieces of that. If that part were more customizable, it would dominate and, I feel, would generate more in the long run because it would maintain more affordability and build more long-term clients, but when you start to crest into the thousand-dollar range, the bang for your buck is lessened.”7 Persona: Roofing Owner. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“The price is a bit high and if you want to utilize some additional features you have to pay substantially more. I was wanting to use the checklist but would need a different plan that was an additional $50 per month. We have a small company so paying around $100 a month is a lot for our budget.”29 Persona: Small Roofing Business / Admin. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“No real scheduling features. Too expensive for what it does. I like the product overall and we do still pay for it. But we don’t buy seats for everyone, only those that really need it.”32 Persona: Roofing Owner. Stage: Use & Succeed.

The price-creep version of the same anger shows up when the bill climbs after the customer is locked in.

“When we first started with JobNimbus it was due to an ok product at a great price. Now many years later and a few price increases all they seem to want to do is integrate.”33 Persona: Roofing VP / Owner. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“The pricing crept up faster than I expected.”36 Persona: Service Contractor / Roofing Adjacent. Stage: Use & Succeed.

1.3 “The mobile app barely works”

Field crews live on their phones, and the category keeps shipping mobile apps that fail them. The complaint is specific and physical: features missing, photos jumbled, the app so bad crews fall back to the desktop browser. It appears across JobNimbus and AccuLynx, a category-wide failure that a roofing-native challenger can win on.

“The TERRIBLE mobile apps (IOS & Android). It’s so bad our field guys have resorted to opening the desktop version on their browsers. The main dashboard and the constant notifications and task reminders are overwhelming instead of being useful.”16 Persona: Field Crew / Tech Lead. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“The mobile app wasn’t our favorite and it could not do what we needed it to do in terms of reporting.”14 Persona: Roofing VP / Operations. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“The app is terrible when it comes to photos. If you are reviewing one of 1051 photos and let’s say you scroll to picture number 45 way at the top when you back out of looking at that photo it starts you over at 1051 again.”1 Persona: Project Manager. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“It’s very challenging to create a report and edit photos for projects. I’ve used it now for 5 years and do find it 4 stars but has areas to be fixed and approved. The photos do not upload in order and are jumbled. Reports are so hard to organize.”42 Persona: Sales / Commercial Roofing. Stage: Use & Succeed.

1.4 “Integration with Quickbooks is a daily challenge”

The accounting sync is where trust breaks. When the books are wrong, the owner’s confidence in the whole platform collapses. This is the operational backbone the arguments name as PitchPro’s strongest near-term wedge, and the reviews show why: incumbents keep getting it wrong, across JobNimbus, AccuLynx, and Housecall Pro.

“Sales tax does not sync properly with qbo! Lets say you put in a tax rate into Jobnimbus and it syncs to qbo… Qbo then records the sales tax as your default global tax rate. Then, it will sync this incorrect tax rate BACK to Jobnimbus and all of your sales tax is messed up. I honestly wish I did not invest so much time and resources into this program.”20 Persona: Roofing Owner / Office Manager. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“Setting up Job Nimbus has been a frustrating ordeal, filled with misleading promises and constant headaches. One example: It would not work with our version of quickbooks even tho we were assured it would. The mobile app barely works—half the features are broken or missing, and the entire system is glitchy and unreliable.”19 Persona: Roofing Owner / Contractor. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“Sync issues and communication between 3rd party systems. We use QBO and at times we have issues with payments and expenses recording accurately.”28 Persona: Accounts Payable / Office. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“Impossible to use HCP to create contracts that are legally binding. Must use docuSign or another application. Invoicing cannot be customized. Integration with Quickbooks is a daily challenge, search function is truly a problem with the new release… this is for a tiny business or for a team who has no software experience.”21 Persona: Roofing Owner / Executive. Stage: Use & Succeed.

1.5 “Measurements are regularly incorrect”

For roofers, a bad measurement is a lost bid or a short order. The measurement vendors draw fire on both accuracy and cost, and the two compound: pay a premium, still get it wrong. This is concentrated on Roofr and EagleView, the measurement specialists, so it is vendor-specific rather than category-wide, but it shapes the cost of every estimate.

“not optimized for mobile use, price increased 50% after we started using it more regularly, costumer service has a coverall approach to every issue (3 free credits), Measurements are regularly incorrect, roof pitches are regularly incorrect (which is a big deal when the difference between 7/12 and 9/12 is $25 per square). Site use is cumbersome and in the end unusable to the vast majority of contractors.”22 Persona: Roofing Owner. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“Reports consistently underestimate the number of shingles needed on a job… a basic shingle report (including waste factor) underestimated the shingles needed by 6 squares!… they wouldn’t tell us the shingles that the premium report would have done for the most recent job. I told her that I needed to have documentation that the more expensive report would have quoted the additional six squares in order to continue doing business with them. She had the Sr. rep escalate and ask their Sr. managers if they would help us as this is a customer service issue and they said no!”23 Persona: Sales / Roofing Company. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“Tree obstructions also caused issues, making us switch about half our reports, and the $15-$38 per report cost felt steep for smaller jobs.”24 Persona: Roof Inspector / Estimator. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“My last 3 request have come back as not being able to get clear imagery!!! Google earth and EagleView both have very clear pictures, why can’t you!!! I did because of the ease and price, BUT HAD TO SWITCH BACK BECAUSE MY LAST 3 REPORTS CAME BACK AS UNAVAILABLE”25 Persona: Roofing Owner. Stage: Use & Succeed.

1.6 “The customer service is a joke”

When something breaks, contractors cannot reach a human, and the silence ends the relationship. Support failure is a recurring churn trigger across CompanyCam, EagleView, and AccuLynx.

“I have had a matter I have been trying to address for over 2 months now and they are unresponsive to the direct issue at hand. the customer service is a joke.”30 Persona: Roofing Owner / Small Business. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“I sat on hold for over an hour today waiting for a customer service representative who never even came to the line… I cancelled the report, drove out to job once again and just took 2 hovers and diagrammed the other structures. For ease of access and fast reports, we are not fans of this platform any longer.. Lost long time customer.”27 Persona: Roofing Owner. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“Support hours. Not available evenings and weekends. We often have issues outside of normal business hours.”40 Persona: Roofing Owner. Stage: Use & Succeed.

1.7 “A very long and hard setup”

Onboarding is where the deal goes cold. Owners discover the system takes months and a dedicated person to stand up, time they do not have. This is the anxiety force at the purchase stage, and it appears most sharply on AccuLynx.

“A very long and hard setup to get everything going. Not recommended if you are already too busy with your company. Bring on someone just to set this up for you.”5 Persona: Office / Admin. Stage: Evaluation.
“We are still at beginning stages and not fully live — The process for us began months ago. I personally do not have the extensive hours to get this running on top of my normal day to day. I was unaware of the all in it would need to get going.”39 Persona: Office / Admin. Stage: Purchase.
“Those integrations are often finicky at best. Nothing flows easily if you don’t have the team to properly onboard the software and manually enter information into each subset. I would compare JobNimbus and its competition to Apple vs. Android. One is very open source while sacrificing its ease of use.”15 Persona: Sales Manager / Roofing SMB. Stage: Use & Succeed.

1.8 “They hold their data hostage”

The exit is where resentment peaks. Customers fear they cannot leave with their own data, and the ones who try report that the promise to hand it back was not kept. This is the data-hostage anxiety, and it surfaces on AccuLynx in the harshest language in the set.

“Avoid at all costs, they are not AI friendly, not customizable, there are many better options in 2026… they hold their data hostage, they think they are doing you a favor by reducing user counts on your billing for users you deactivate… utter trash”8 Persona: Roofing Owner. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“They promise they could retrieve all my files in case we cancel in a quick easy download. That did not happen. The only part they sent was the data. We decided to cancel because they nickel and dime for very basic features such as docusigns, using their portals for client services, etc.”10 Persona: Roofing Owner. Stage: Use & Succeed.

2. Emotional Undercurrent

The reviews carry named emotions, not vague dissatisfaction. Reading them in sequence, four registers dominate.

Resignation. The most common register is a customer who stays while quietly hating it. Melanie M. captures the trapped feeling exactly: she stays because nothing else consolidates her work, and she resents the standstill.

“I stay because I haven’t found anything else that integrates project management, crm and a calendar in one software. But I consider leaving often with the lack of progress on the development side… Some requests, that really are basic, have been sitting out there for YEARS with no response on if and when the development team will do them.”17 Persona: Office / Estimating. Stage: Use & Succeed.

Regret. A sharper register: the customer who blames himself for the money and time sunk in. The QuickBooks reviewer ends on it: “I honestly wish I did not invest so much time and resources into this program.”20

A plea for rescue. Some reviews read as open appeals to the market to build something better. Sonja M. is begging:

“Please Please Please someone make a comparable product so I can get away from company cam. Horrible customer service. When we had issues it was days before we received a response. our data was unavailable and when the updates were complete we had to relearn the entire application.”34 Persona: Roofing Company Director / Operations. Stage: Use & Succeed.
  • Contempt, controlled. At the exit, the language turns cold and final. “utter trash”8 and “the customer service is a joke”30 read as verdicts delivered after the decision to leave is already made.

The buyer-versus-user emotional split. The owner who signs the contract and the crew member who uses the app every day feel opposite things, and they say so. The owner’s register is financial and contractual: add-on bills, price creep, data-hostage fear at the exit. Dennis C. counts dollars: “when you start to crest into the thousand-dollar range, the bang for your buck is lessened.”7 The field user’s register is physical and daily: the photo viewer that dumps him back to the top, the app so broken his crew opens a desktop browser instead. Michael C. is exasperated at a single broken scroll, “it starts you over at 1051 again,”1 a problem the owner will never see and the crew hits fifty times a day. The owner wants a predictable bill and a clean exit. The user wants a phone app that does not fight him. A single message cannot satisfy both, so the messaging must segment: owner-facing copy leads on predictable cost and ownership of your own data; field-facing copy leads on a mobile app and photo handling that actually work.

3. Why They Hired

The reviews trace a recognizable path from first thought to habit. The personas here match the Ideal Customer Profile used across the other deliverables, the $2 to $10M roofing contractor and the people inside that business.

First thought and awareness. The triggering thought is almost always the same: I am sick of jumping between apps to finish one job. Christina S. names the before-state that starts the search.

“Sometimes, using multiple software applications, to try and get one job done can be frustrating. Not so with HouseCall Pro.”37 Persona: Office Manager. Stage: Awareness.

Active evaluation. Once they start looking, roofers screen for whether the tool is built for roofing rather than generic field service. Esther B. switched for that reason.

“It was limited in roofing specific integrations”38 Persona: Office Manager / Roofing. Stage: Evaluation.

The decision driver: speed wins the job. In storm work, the contractor who hands over a professional proposal first usually books the work. This is the pull force that makes speed-to-proposal a buying trigger.

“In a storm market, the contractor who puts a professional proposal in a homeowner’s hands first usually wins the job. For storm restoration estimators running 8–12 inspections a day during a surge, that speed is a direct competitive advantage.”41 Persona: Storm Restoration Contractor. Stage: Awareness.

First use and habit. The platforms that earn a permanent place do so by collapsing the app stack into one system, the consolidation pull that turns a trial into a habit.

“I like that AccuLynx keeps all jobs, leads, documents, photos, notes, and communication inside one system. It eliminates the chaos of using multiple apps and keeps the whole team aligned.”9 Persona: Sales Manager / Roofing. Stage: Use & Succeed.

The desire to reduce the app count is so strong that customers ask their existing vendors to absorb the tools they still pay for elsewhere.

“Would love for them to add a timeclock (already have the gps and job information to avoid another app). Checklists with email notifications to other users would be a great addition as well.”39 Persona: Roofing Owner. Stage: Use & Succeed.

4. Why They Almost Didn’t

The forces working against a switch are as clear in the reviews as the forces pulling toward one. Four show up repeatedly.

  1. Push. The pain of the current tool is what gets a roofer to look at all: double entry, broken mobile, QuickBooks errors, add-on bills. Section 1 holds that catalog in the customers’ own words. It is the strongest force in the set and the one PitchPro inherits for free from the category.
  2. Anxiety about setup. The fear that gets loudest at the buying moment is onboarding. Roofers have watched peers lose months to a setup they did not budget for. Andrea A. lived it: “The process for us began months ago. I personally do not have the extensive hours to get this running on top of my normal day to day. I was unaware of the all in it would need to get going.”39 The reviews that warn “Bring on someone just to set this up for you”5 are the objection a PitchPro sales conversation has to defuse on the first call.
  3. Anxiety about the exit. The second fear is getting trapped. Eugene C. names the broken promise that confirms it: “They promise they could retrieve all my files in case we cancel in a quick easy download. That did not happen.”10 A prospect reading reviews sees this and assumes every vendor does it. PitchPro has to say the opposite out loud and mean it.
  4. Habit. The strongest reason customers do not switch is that they already have everything in one place and dread rebuilding it elsewhere. Melanie M. is the portrait of habit beating dissatisfaction: she “consider[s] leaving often” and stays anyway because no alternative consolidates her work.17 Habit is why a better product alone is not enough. The switch has to feel safe and fast, or the incumbent keeps the customer by inertia.

5. Language Gap

The left column is verbatim customer language from the reviews. The middle column is the category cliché vendors reach for, stated at the category level and never drawn from PitchPro’s own copy. The right column is the shift PitchPro should make.

What customers say What vendors say The shift to make
“I have to double enter everything”18 “all-in-one platform” Name the specific job: enter a quote once and watch it flow to QuickBooks without retyping.
“Everything is an add-on… when you start to crest into the thousand-dollar range, the bang for your buck is lessened”7 “flexible plans,” “scalable pricing” Say the price and what it includes: one bill, no per-seat tax, no add-on surprises.
“The mobile app barely works”19 manage your business from anywhere Promise the field reality: a phone app your crew will actually use, photos that stay in order.
“Integration with Quickbooks is a daily challenge”21 “seamless integrations” Claim the clean books: two-way QuickBooks sync that keeps your tax rates and totals right.
“A very long and hard setup… Bring on someone just to set this up for you”5 “easy onboarding” Promise the runway: built to run itself, set up without a dedicated hire or lost months.
“they hold their data hostage”8 (silence: vendors do not address the exit) Address it head-on: your data is yours, and you can leave with it.

Words to adopt: roofing-native; runs itself; one bill; no add-on surprises; your data is yours; enter it once; works on the phone; lead to QuickBooks.

Words to drop: all-in-one; seamless; scalable; flexible plans; manage from anywhere; powerful; robust; end-to-end.

6. Unvoiced Complaints

Some grievances sit underneath the reviews without being stated outright. They are inferred only from patterns the quotes support.

  1. Customers expect to be ignored after they sign. The reviews about requests “sitting out there for YEARS”17 and a vendor with “no incentive to make sweeping improvements”11 point to a quieter assumption: that once the contract is signed, the customer stops mattering. Nobody writes that they expect to be abandoned. The resignation in the language says it for them. A vendor that visibly fixes what customers report would land against a market that has stopped expecting to be heard.
  2. The setup pain is partly shame. Andrea A.’s “I was unaware of the all in it would need to get going”39 carries self-blame, not just frustration. Buyers feel they should have known better, so they under-report how badly onboarding went and over-warn the next buyer. The unvoiced fear is looking foolish for choosing wrong. Messaging that removes the setup burden also removes that risk of public regret.
  3. They quietly assume roofing-specific is a luxury. Esther B. switched because a tool was “limited in roofing specific integrations,”38 but most reviewers tolerate generic field-service software as the normal cost of doing business. The unvoiced complaint is that they have settled for tools not built for them. A roofing-native product names a need they have stopped asking out loud.

7. Rave Notes

This is the testimonial bank a copywriter would normally pull from. For PitchPro it is empty.

There are no PitchPro raves to bank. PitchPro is pre-revenue with no customers, and a public search of G2, Capterra, GetApp, Trustpilot, Reddit, Product Hunt, Hacker News, and press archives found no third-party reviews, ratings, testimonials, or press mentions of the roofing-software PitchPro.44 No competitor’s praise belongs here. Repurposing a rival’s testimonial as a PitchPro rave would be both dishonest and useless to a copywriter.

As PitchPro’s first trial customers come online, collect permission-cleared testimonials from them and build this bank for real: capture the verbatim, secure written permission to publish, and tag each quote by the theme it proves (consolidation, predictable price, mobile that works, clean QuickBooks, fast setup). The primary-interview program in Section 8 is the vehicle to gather them. This section stays empty by design until those quotes exist.

8. Strategic Implications

Each implication below turns customer voice into an action tied to a Brief recommendation or an MMG pillar. The first is the precondition for trusting all the others.

Commission a primary-interview and case-study program with the first trial customers. This report runs on public reviews of competitors because PitchPro has no buyers to interview yet.45 Stand up AI-led custom interviews the moment trial customers activate, and use them to confirm or sharpen the four things the public record can only guess: the emotional registers at purchase, the buyer-versus-user split named in Section 2, the real first-thought-to-first-use timeline, and the forces that win or lose the deal. This converts an inherited category map into PitchPro’s own evidence. Commission it.

Lead every surface with roofing-native, runs-itself automation, and retire “all-in-one.” Customers never say “all-in-one,” and four of the top six competitors already do, so the phrase differentiates nothing.46 They say “I have to double enter everything”18 and they praise the system that “eliminates the chaos of using multiple apps.”9 This reinforces the Brief’s spine recommendation and the lead MMG pillar. Replace the breadth superlative with the specific roofing-native, runs-itself promise across every surface. Rewrite it.

Pour the message into the operational backbone competitors keep breaking. The QuickBooks sync that records the wrong tax rate,20 the mobile app crews abandon for a browser,16 and the photo viewer that dumps you back to the top1 are the live, well-evidenced gaps. This is the strongest near-term wedge in the arguments and an MMG proof pillar. Claim clean two-way QuickBooks, a real mobile experience, and ordered photos as headline promises, and ship the engineering behind them. Anchor the brand there.

Make predictable cost and no add-on surprises the pricing message, not “cheapest.” The category rage is at being nickel-and-dimed: “Everything is an add-on,”7 “$50 per month” for a checklist,29 price that “crept up faster than I expected.”36 Roofr is already flat and cheaper at entry, so cheapest is not winnable. This ties to the Brief’s pricing recommendation. Lead pricing on one predictable bill, no per-seat tax, no add-on surprises, and never on headline price. Position it.

Segment the message by buyer versus user. The owner fears the bill and the exit; the field crew fights the app. They want opposite things and say so.7 1 This sharpens the MMG persona work. Write owner-facing copy on predictable cost and data ownership, and field-facing copy on a mobile app and photo handling that work. Split it.

Defuse the setup and data-exit fears in the sales conversation, not just the marketing. Onboarding anxiety (“Bring on someone just to set this up for you”5) and exit fear (“they hold their data hostage”8) are the two objections that kill the deal at the buying moment. This connects to the Brief’s customer-facing fixes. Build first-call talk-tracks that promise fast self-running setup and your-data-is-yours portability, and prove both in the trial. Address them.

Treat the review surface as a sales channel: reviews are the new sales floor. Every quote in this report is a prospect reading the same public reviews mid-evaluation, on Capterra, G2, and the App Store, before they ever talk to a vendor. The reviews are doing the selling, for and against, while the company is absent from the conversation. As PitchPro earns its first reviews, monitor those surfaces, respond to every review in public, and route permission-cleared raves into the Section 7 bank. This connects to the demand-capture recommendation in the Brief. Own the review surface as a managed sales channel. Manage it.

Sources

  • Capterra, AccuLynx, Michael C., Project Manager, Construction 51-200 employees, November 12 2025, https://www.capterra.com/p/116187/Acculynx/reviews/
  • Capterra, AccuLynx, Andrea A., Admin, Construction, December 3 2025, https://www.capterra.com/p/116187/Acculynx/reviews/
  • Capterra, AccuLynx, dennis C., owner, Construction, November 11 2024, https://www.capterra.com/p/116187/Acculynx/reviews/
  • Capterra, AccuLynx, Jay Z., Founder, Construction, June 10 2026, https://www.capterra.com/p/116187/Acculynx/reviews/?page=3
  • Capterra, AccuLynx, Justin H., Sales Manager, Construction, December 3 2025, https://www.capterra.com/p/116187/Acculynx/reviews/?page=5
  • G2, AccuLynx, Eugene C., President, Small Business, https://www.g2.com/products/acculynx/reviews
  • Capterra, AccuLynx, Megan T., Office Manager, Construction, April 3 2025, https://www.capterra.com/p/116187/Acculynx/reviews/?page=5
  • Capterra, JobNimbus, Brandon B., Commercial Vice President, Construction 11-50 employees, April 9 2024, https://www.capterra.com/p/126797/JobNimbus/reviews/
  • Capterra, JobNimbus, Julia C., VP Operations, Construction 11-50 employees, November 11 2024, https://www.capterra.com/p/126797/JobNimbus/reviews/
  • Capterra, JobNimbus, Rafael P., Sales Manager, Construction, March 29 2023, https://www.capterra.com/p/126797/JobNimbus/reviews/
  • Capterra, JobNimbus, Verified Reviewer, Tech & Development, Construction, August 30 2019, https://www.capterra.com/p/126797/JobNimbus/reviews/
  • Capterra, JobNimbus, Melanie M., Estimating Assistant, Construction, May 4 2020, https://www.capterra.com/p/126797/JobNimbus/reviews/
  • Capterra, JobNimbus, Daniel G., Production Manager, Construction, August 28 2019, https://www.capterra.com/p/126797/JobNimbus/reviews/
  • Apple iOS App Store, JobNimbus All-In-One Roof App, reviewer 9393948483929293847474, July 11 2025, https://apps.apple.com/us/app/jobnimbus-all-in-one-roof-app/id1571207100
  • Apple iOS App Store, JobNimbus All-In-One Roof App, reviewer hbsozyvvIhcy, December 21 2023, https://apps.apple.com/us/app/jobnimbus-all-in-one-roof-app/id1571207100

  • Roofing Software Guide editorial research, roofingsoftwareguide.com (industry observer citing contractor behavior patterns), 2025

  • PitchPro external-proof search (G2, Capterra, GetApp, Trustpilot, Reddit, Product Hunt, Hacker News, press archives), no third-party reviews or testimonials located, 2026
  • Research methodology note, PitchPro is pre-revenue with no customers, no first-party customer interviews; VoC built from public sources
  • AccuLynx homepage (https://acculynx.com/); JobNimbus roofing page (https://www.jobnimbus.dev/industries/roofing-software/); RoofingSoftwareGuide ‘Best Roofing Software 2026’ (https://roofingsoftwareguide.com/roundups/best-roofing-software/); projul.com (https://projul.com/industries/roofing/)
Fenn & Hart
